best_for: Small-to-mid teams shipping continuously, especially with feature flags to hide incomplete work — the default recommendation for most of this skill's target projects
avoid_when: Large teams with long release-certification cycles (regulated industries) that genuinely need a stabilization branch
tradeoffs: Fast integration, minimal merge conflicts, forces small PRs — but requires feature flags or very short-lived branches to hide incomplete work from production

best_for: Teams shipping scheduled releases (not continuous deploy), or software with a real 'release candidate' testing phase before general availability
avoid_when: Web apps deploying continuously — the extra branch overhead has no payoff when every merge to main ships within minutes anyway
tradeoffs: Clear separation between in-progress and released code, at the cost of merge overhead and integration delay compared to trunk-based

option: Lint + Typecheck + Test + Build as Required PR Checks
best_for: Every project, without exception — these four gates catch the overwhelming majority of preventable regressions before merge
avoid_when: Never skip — even a solo-developer project benefits from catching type errors and broken builds before they reach main
tradeoffs: Every PR is provably lint-clean, type-safe, tested, and buildable before merge — the floor, not the ceiling, of a CI pipeline

best_for: Any CI pipeline installing npm/pip/etc. packages — cache the package manager's store keyed on the lockfile hash
avoid_when: A pipeline with so few runs that caching setup cost isn't recouped — rare in practice once a team has more than a couple PRs a week
tradeoffs: Cuts CI run time significantly (often the single biggest CI speed win), at the cost of occasionally needing to bust a stale/corrupted cache

best_for: Libraries or tools that must support multiple runtime versions/platforms and need to verify compatibility on every PR
avoid_when: A single-deployment-target app where you control the exact runtime version in production — testing a matrix of versions you'll never actually run adds CI time for no real signal
tradeoffs: Catches version-specific compatibility bugs before users hit them, at a real CI-minutes cost multiplied by the matrix size

best_for: Monorepos of meaningful size (Turborepo/Nx/pnpm workspaces) where running the full test suite on every PR regardless of what changed wastes significant CI time
avoid_when: A small monorepo where the full suite already runs in under a minute or two — the affected-package detection setup isn't worth it yet
tradeoffs: Dramatically faster CI as the monorepo grows, at the cost of needing correct dependency-graph tooling (Turborepo/Nx) to determine 'affected' correctly

option: Continuous Deployment (every merge to main auto-deploys)
best_for: Small-to-mid teams with strong CI gates (DO003) and feature flags for anything not ready for users — the default for most SaaS/agency-site projects on this skill's default stack
avoid_when: Regulated industries requiring a formal, auditable release-approval step before production changes
tradeoffs: Fastest feedback loop from merge to production, but requires trusting the CI gate completely — there's no human 'are you sure' step by design

option: Manual Approval Gate Before Production Deploy
best_for: Regulated environments, high-stakes production systems, or teams not yet confident enough in their CI test coverage to fully trust continuous deployment
avoid_when: Teams that have this gate purely as a rubber-stamp with no real review happening — that's continuous deployment with extra latency and no actual safety benefit
tradeoffs: Adds a deliberate human checkpoint before production changes, at the cost of deploy latency and a real person needing to be available to approve

best_for: Any team where reviewing a PR benefits from seeing it actually running (frontend changes especially) rather than just reading a diff — Vercel/Netlify/Cloudflare Pages all support this natively
avoid_when: A backend-only change with no visual/behavioral surface a reviewer would benefit from clicking through
tradeoffs: Reviewers test the real thing instead of imagining it from a diff, catching visual/UX issues before merge — essentially free on platforms that support it natively (part of the reason Vercel/Netlify/Cloudflare are the default recommendation)

best_for: Traditional server/container deployments (not serverless/edge) where you want zero-downtime cutover and instant rollback by flipping traffic back to the old environment
avoid_when: Serverless/edge platforms (Vercel, Cloudflare Workers) — they handle this pattern for you automatically; don't hand-roll it there
tradeoffs: Instant rollback (just flip traffic back) and zero-downtime cutover, at the cost of running two full production environments simultaneously during the switch

best_for: High-traffic production systems where you want to catch a bad deploy's impact on a small percentage of traffic before it reaches everyone
avoid_when: Low-traffic apps where a canary population is too small to produce a statistically meaningful signal before you'd have just rolled back anyway
tradeoffs: Limits blast radius of a bad deploy to a small traffic percentage, at the cost of needing real-time metrics/alerting sensitive enough to detect a problem in a canary slice

option: Feature Flags Instead of Long-Lived Branches
best_for: Any trunk-based-development team (DO001) — merge incomplete work behind a flag, deploy continuously, toggle the feature on when ready
avoid_when: A one-line, low-risk change with no real 'incomplete state' to hide — flagging that is unnecessary ceremony
tradeoffs: Decouples 'deployed' from 'released to users,' enabling true continuous deployment without exposing half-finished features — but flags left un-cleaned-up become tech debt of their own

option: Migrations Run as an Automated Pipeline Step
best_for: Every project with a database — migrations apply automatically as part of the deploy pipeline (see DB064), triggered after a successful build/test but before traffic shifts to the new version
avoid_when: Never skip automating this — manual migration application is exactly the kind of error-prone, unaudited step CI/CD exists to eliminate
tradeoffs: Consistent, auditable, and repeatable across environments, but requires the zero-downtime migration discipline from database-schema-design.md so a migration mid-rollout doesn't break in-flight requests

best_for: Every production deploy — hit a handful of critical endpoints/pages immediately after deploy and auto-rollback (or alert) if they fail
avoid_when: A deploy target with no meaningful 'is it actually working' check available (rare) — even a single health-check endpoint hit is better than nothing
tradeoffs: Catches a deploy that passed CI but fails against real production config/environment (missing env var, DNS issue, etc.) within seconds instead of waiting for a user report

option: Automatic Rollback on Failed Health Check
best_for: Platforms/pipelines that support it (most managed platforms do) — configure an automatic rollback if the post-deploy health check fails, rather than requiring a human to notice and act
avoid_when: A deploy pipeline with no reliable health-check signal to trigger on — build that first, then add auto-rollback on top of it
tradeoffs: Fastest possible recovery from a bad deploy (seconds, not however long until a human notices), but requires trusting the health check's accuracy — a flaky health check causes false-positive rollbacks

best_for: Libraries, packages, or APIs with external consumers who need to understand what changed between versions
avoid_when: Internal-only apps deploying continuously with no external version consumers — a changelog nobody reads for an app with no versioned releases is overhead without payoff
tradeoffs: Consumers can assess upgrade risk from the version number alone (major = breaking), and automated changelog generation (from commit messages/PR titles) removes the manual changelog-writing burden

best_for: Every project — security patches for dependencies should never wait for someone to remember to check for them manually
avoid_when: Never skip entirely, though tune the frequency/scope (e.g. security-only auto-merge, feature updates batched weekly) to avoid PR-review fatigue
tradeoffs: Keeps dependencies current with minimal manual effort, especially for security patches, at the cost of some PR-review overhead that needs its own triage policy (auto-merge patch versions, review major versions)

option: CI Secrets Stored in the Platform's Secret Store, Never in the Repo
best_for: Every CI pipeline — API keys, deploy tokens, and database credentials used during CI/CD live in GitHub Actions Secrets / GitLab CI Variables / the platform's equivalent, never committed or hardcoded in a workflow file
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — a secret committed to a repo (even a private one) should be treated as compromised and rotated immediately
tradeoffs: Keeps credentials out of git history entirely; pair with least-privilege scoping (a CI deploy token should only be able to do what the pipeline actually needs)

best_for: Monorepos hosting multiple independently-deployable apps (a web app + an admin panel + a marketing site) where each should deploy on its own schedule based on what actually changed
avoid_when: A monorepo where everything genuinely deploys together as one unit — added complexity with no benefit there
tradeoffs: Each app deploys only when its own code (or shared dependencies it uses) changes, avoiding unnecessary redeploys of unrelated apps, at the cost of needing correct change-detection tooling

option: Run the Full Test Suite Before Any Deploy Step Starts
best_for: Every pipeline — deployment steps are gated strictly behind a fully green test suite, never run in parallel with or before tests complete
avoid_when: Never skip this ordering — deploying before tests finish defeats the entire purpose of having CI tests as a gate
tradeoffs: Guarantees nothing broken by the test suite ever reaches production, at the cost of total pipeline duration being test-time-plus-deploy-time rather than running them in parallel

option: Declare Infrastructure in Version-Controlled Config, Not Manual Dashboard Clicks
best_for: Any infrastructure beyond what a fully-managed platform (Vercel/Netlify/Supabase) auto-provisions — Terraform, Pulumi, or the platform's own config-as-code (vercel.json, wrangler.toml)
avoid_when: A fully-managed platform where the platform's own git-integrated config (vercel.json, netlify.toml) already covers 100% of the needed configuration — that config-in-repo already satisfies this principle
tradeoffs: Infrastructure changes are reviewable, versioned, and reproducible instead of tribal knowledge trapped in a dashboard someone configured once

option: Deploy and CI-Failure Notifications to a Team Channel
best_for: Every team of more than one person — a failed deploy or broken main branch should be visible immediately, not discovered hours later
avoid_when: A true solo project where the developer is already watching the CI status directly after every push
tradeoffs: Keeps the whole team aware of build/deploy health in real time, preventing a broken main branch from sitting unnoticed while others build on top of it

option: Incremental/Cached Builds (Next.js, Turborepo remote caching, etc.)
best_for: Any project where build time has become a real friction point in the deploy loop or local development
avoid_when: Small projects where build time is already under a few seconds — caching infrastructure adds complexity without a corresponding time payoff yet
tradeoffs: Can cut build times dramatically for large projects/monorepos by reusing previous build outputs for unchanged code, at the cost of occasional cache-invalidation bugs that produce a stale build

best_for: Managed platforms (Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare Pages) where every deploy is an immutable, addressable artifact — rolling back means pointing traffic at a previous deploy, not reverting code and redeploying
avoid_when: Traditional infrastructure with no immutable-deploy-artifact concept — there, a git revert + redeploy is the rollback mechanism instead
tradeoffs: Fastest possible rollback (seconds — just repoint traffic) on platforms that support it, since no rebuild is needed at all

best_for: Platforms/infrastructure without an immutable-previous-deploy mechanism, or when the rollback needs to also revert a database migration alongside the code
avoid_when: Managed platforms with instant deploy-rollback available — that's strictly faster when a data/migration revert isn't also needed
tradeoffs: Works everywhere regardless of platform sophistication, but is slower than repointing to an existing artifact since it requires a full rebuild
